does: This paper introduces SAGE, a framework for automatically generating diverse question variants that test the same knowledge in different forms, enabling large-scale robustness augmentation of existing LLM evaluation benchmarks. The approach comprises two main components: VariantQual, a rubric-based verifier trained on human-labeled data to assess variant quality, and VariantGen, a variant generator initialized with supervised fine-tuning and optimized through reinforcement learning using VariantQual as a reward signal. Experiments on HellaSwag demonstrate that SAGE produces robustness-augmented benchmarks at scale with quality comparable to human-annotated variants, achieving this through efficient fine-tuned smaller models rather than expensive large-model pipelines.
connects: This work addresses the brittleness of LLM knowledge evaluation highlighted by recent research showing models struggle with paraphrased or reformulated versions of the same questions. It builds on existing work in benchmark augmentation and adversarial robustness testing while responding to limitations in current generate-then-verify pipelines that rely on expensive LLM inference and suffer from low-yield generation. The paper combines established techniques in supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning—previously used for other NLP tasks—with rubric-based verification, drawing on quality assessment methods developed for text generation evaluation. This extends prior work on LLM-assisted dataset construction by making the process scalable through smaller model specialization.
enables: By enabling efficient, large-scale generation of knowledge evaluation variants, this work supports development of more robust LLM evaluation practices and could facilitate creation of broader robustness-augmented benchmarks across multiple domains beyond HellaSwag. The approach points toward systematic testing of LLM knowledge stability and opens avenues for understanding how question reformulation affects model performance. The framework's reliance on fine-tuned smaller models rather than large-scale generation suggests broader implications for scalable benchmark construction. Future work could extend this to other knowledge domains, explore what types of variants prove most challenging for models, and investigate whether training on such augmented benchmarks improves downstream robustness in real-world applications.
